# Arbiter

> Open-source MCP tool-call firewall. Arbiter governs what agents are allowed to do. It does not govern what agents try to do. It protects the platform by reducing the tool-call attack surface.

Sits between AI agents and MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers, enforcing deny-by-default authorization, session budgets, drift detection, and structured auditing on every tool call.

## Key Facts

- Language: Rust
- License: Apache-2.0
- Author: Cyrene I.
- Repository: https://github.com/cyrenei/arbiter
- Documentation: https://cyrenei.github.io/arbiter-mcp-firewall/docs/
- Homepage: https://cyrenei.github.io/arbiter-mcp-firewall/

## What Arbiter Does

Every tool call passes through a 9-stage middleware pipeline:

1. TLS termination
2. OAuth 2.1 JWT validation with JWKS caching and multi-issuer support
3. Agent identity resolution from verified claims
4. Time-limited, budget-capped task session binding
5. Deny-by-default, specificity-ordered policy evaluation
6. Drift detection (flags when operation types diverge from session scope)
7. Credential injection for authorized calls
8. Reverse proxying to upstream MCP server
9. Structured JSONL audit logging with PII redaction

## Core Capabilities

- Agent identity and delegation with trust levels and cascade deactivation
- Deny-by-default authorization: policy engine evaluating agent identity, session context, tool name, and parameter constraints
- Task sessions: time-limited, budget-capped, tool-whitelisted sessions per task
- Drift detection: flags or blocks when operation types diverge from session scope
- Per-agent session cap to prevent session multiplication attacks
- Response body inspection for secrets, PII, and prompt injection payloads
- Prometheus metrics: request counts, latency histograms, active sessions gauge
- Environment-based secrets management with constant-time comparison

## Who Arbiter Is For

Engineering teams that deploy AI agents using MCP and need deny-by-default authorization on individual tool calls, not just API endpoints.

## Who Arbiter Is Not For

Teams requiring centralized NHI management across hundreds of service identities, or those looking for a managed SaaS platform.

## Architecture

Arbiter is a Rust workspace with 14 specialized crates: arbiter (integration binary), arbiter-proxy, arbiter-oauth, arbiter-identity, arbiter-lifecycle, arbiter-cli, arbiter-mcp, arbiter-policy, arbiter-session, arbiter-behavior, arbiter-metrics, arbiter-audit, arbiter-credential, arbiter-storage.
